title: Decoherence due to the sudden coupling of an impurity to a metal
authors: Felipe D. Picoli, Gustavo Diniz, Luiz N. Oliveira
abstract: We investigate the nonequilibrium dynamics and loss of coherence in a quantum impurity system using the spinless resonant level model subject to sudden quenches of the hybridization between the impurity and the metal. The survival probability (fidelity) and impurity occupation are analyzed as probes of the dephasing induced by particle-hole excitations. For finite systems, the loss of coherence loss is only apparent, as discrete spectra lead to quasi-periodic dynamics and revivals when phases realign. We show that a mixed linear-logarithmic discretization suppresses these finite-size artifacts by rendering excitation energies incommensurate, thereby reducing revivals. Starting from the exactly solvable two-level limit exhibiting coherent Rabi oscillations, we extend the analysis to large lattices, where damping and relaxation emerge. Combining analytical and numerical results, we provide a unified picture of the crossover from coherent oscillations to effectively irreversible decoherence.
summary: This paper explores how suddenly coupling an impurity to a metal leads to the decay of quantum coherence. It demonstrates that while small systems show artificial revivals, using advanced numerical methods like eNRG reveals a true transition to irreversible decoherence driven by particle-hole excitations.
main_problem: Investigating the nonequilibrium dynamics and the loss of quantum coherence (dephasing) in a quantum impurity system following sudden quenches of the coupling to a metal.
main_result: The study establishes a unified picture of the crossover from coherent Rabi oscillations to irreversible decoherence, identifying the Anderson orthogonality catastrophe and Nozières-De Dominicis mechanisms as drivers of power-law decay in fidelity.
method: The authors combine analytical solutions for the single-site limit with numerical approaches, specifically using the embedded Numerical Renormalization Group (eNRG) and exact diagonalization to suppress finite-size artifacts.
model_or_system: A spinless resonant level model consisting of a single impurity orbital coupled to a tight-binding metal lattice via a time-dependent hybridization term.
key_observables: Quantum fidelity (survival probability), impurity occupation, and phase shifts at the Fermi level.
important_parameters: Impurity energy (epsilon_d), hybridization strength (Gamma), lattice size (L), and the hopping parameter (tau).
main_assumptions: The study is restricted to an uncorrelated (noninteracting) Hamiltonian and focuses on the small coupling regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows impurity occupation and fidelity for switching coupling on vs. off; Figure 2 displays non-monotonic decay of occupation and monotonic decay of fidelity; Figure 3 shows fidelity decay for different hybridization strengths; Figure 4 shows damped oscillations in occupation; Figure 5 compares eNRG accuracy against flawed tight-binding diagonalization.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the single-site limit, moves to analyzing finite-size effects and discretization techniques, presents the results for sudden quenches (on and off), and concludes with a discussion of the physical mechanisms of decoherence and limitations of the model.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into the fundamental mechanisms of dephasing and the transition from unitary to dissipative dynamics in open quantum systems, specifically through the lens of the orthogonality catastrophe.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(8)].

All curves computed with impurity energy ϵd = −0.3 τ and width Γ = 0.3 τ. The fidelities have been scaled by the Doniach-Sunjic power law with an exponent α = 2( δ

Figure 4 Impurity occupation following a sudden quench, for the impurity energy ϵd = −0.3τ and the indicated hybridizations Γ. When the coupling is switched off, the impurity occupation becomes constant, as illustrated by the green and the blue horizontal lines in the two panels. Upon switch-on, the occupation oscillates with frequency ϵd

Figure 3 Decay of the quantum fidelity after the coupling between the impurity and the metal is suddenly turned on or off, for the indicated hybridizations Γ. For each Γ the behaviors after the sudden switch-on and switch-off are shown. In all cases, the fidelity decay follows the Doniach- Sunjic law, the exponent α [Eq. (41)] increasing with Γ. The contributions to the fidelity from the electronic transitions between the impurity and the metal decay are governed by the Nozi`eres-De Dominicis exponent β [Eq(42)] and decay faster, progressively reducing the interference and therefore damping the the oscillations.

Figure 5 Fidelity as a function of time for a metal de- scribed by a tight-binding Hamiltonian, coupled to an impurity with energy ϵd = −0.3τ and hybridization Γ = 0.1τ. The coupling between the metal and impurity is turned on at t = 0. The red curve represents the essentially exact fidelity computed by the eNRG large ζ procedure, with λ = 1.5, ζ = 980 and M = 1000. The blue curve in close agreement with this benchmark is the result of an eNRG computation with λ = 1.5, ζ = 190, and M = 10. The green curve resulted directly from the diagonalization of the model Hamiltonian, Eq. (1).
